tracks.title: Foundations
tracks.subtitle: From zero to your first real shots

lessons.title: Know Your Clubs
lessons.subtitle: What's in the bag and what each one does

steps.title: Watch: a tour of the bag
steps.body: A 2-minute walk through every club type and when you'd pull it. The full written guide follows.

steps.title: The bag, club by club
steps.body: Every club trades distance for control. Lower numbers = longer shafts, less loft, more distance, harder to hit. Higher numbers and wedges = shorter, more loft, more control.
points: DRIVER — the longest club, hit off a tee. Biggest head, least loft (~10°). Distance king, accuracy diva.
WOODS (3W, 5W) — long shots from the tee or fairway. Easier than driver, still plenty of distance.
HYBRIDS — wood-shaped rescue clubs that replace hard-to-hit long irons (3i/4i). Very forgiving.
IRONS (5–9) — the scoring workhorses. 5-iron goes far and low; 9-iron goes short and high. One club ≈ 10–12 yards of gap.
WEDGES (PW, GW, SW, LW) — 50–130 yards and everything around the green. The sand wedge is built to splash out of bunkers.
PUTTER — the flat one. You'll use it more than any other club in the bag.
Starting out? You only need: driver OR 3-wood, a hybrid, 7-iron, 9-iron, sand wedge, putter.

quiz.question: Which goes FARTHER: a 5-iron or a 9-iron?
quiz.why: Lower number = longer shaft + less loft = more distance.

quiz.question: Which club is built for bunkers?
quiz.why: The sand wedge's wide sole slides through sand instead of digging.

lessons.title: Hold the Club
lessons.subtitle: Grip is 80% of your ball flight

steps.title: Why grip comes first
steps.body: Your hands are the only part of you touching the club. The grip controls the club face, and the face controls where the ball starts and curves. Get this right now and every later lesson gets easier.
points: The club face sends the ball; your grip steers the face
A neutral grip lets your body swing free without hand corrections
Learn one grip and keep it — consistency beats perfection

steps.title: The neutral grip, in 3D
steps.body: Rotate the model. Work through each checkpoint on your own club.
checkpoints: Lead hand: club in the fingers, not the palm
See 2 knuckles of your lead hand looking down
Trail hand: lifeline covers the lead thumb
Pinky interlocks or overlaps the lead index finger
Pressure 4/10 — hold a bird, don't crush it

steps.title: Show me your grip
steps.body: Hold your grip up in front of the camera. We'll check hand stacking with on-device hand tracking — or step through the checklist yourself.
checkpoints: Hands touching, no gap between them
Thumbs pointing mostly down the shaft

quiz.question: Where should the club sit in your lead hand?
quiz.why: Fingers let the wrists hinge; palms lock them up.

quiz.question: How many lead-hand knuckles should you see at address?
quiz.why: Two knuckles = neutral. More = strong (hook bias), fewer = weak (slice bias).

steps.title: Posture powers the swing
steps.body: A golf swing is a rotation around your spine. Set the spine angle at address and keep it — most contact problems are posture problems in disguise.
points: DISTANCE TO BALL: arms hang straight down from your shoulders, then grip — if you're reaching or cramped, step in or out. Longer club = stand farther away, ball more forward.
Feet shoulder-width; ball position center for short irons, one ball forward per longer club, off the lead heel for driver
Hinge from the hips (~30–40°), soft knees — butt back, chest over the ball
More bend for wedges (shortest shafts), tallest posture for driver

steps.title: Address position, in 3D
steps.body: Match each checkpoint in a mirror or the camera.

steps.title: Hold your address
steps.body: Set up the phone face-on, take your address, and hold still for 3 seconds. We'll measure your spine tilt.

quiz.question: Where do you hinge from at address?
quiz.why: Hip hinge keeps the spine straight and the arms free.

lessons.title: Aim & Alignment
lessons.subtitle: Train tracks: body left, face at the target

steps.title: Aim is a system, not a glance
steps.body: The club face aims AT the target. Your feet, hips and shoulders aim PARALLEL-LEFT of it (right-handers) — like train tracks. Most 'slices' start as alignment errors.
points: Pick an intermediate spot 2 feet ahead of the ball on your line
Set the face to the spot FIRST, then build your stance
Lay a club on the ground along your toes to practice

quiz.question: Where should your feet aim?
quiz.why: Body parallel-left, face at the target — train tracks.

steps.title: One piece back
steps.body: Hands, arms and chest move away together — the 'one-piece takeaway'. No wrist flick, no sway. When the club head passes your trail foot it should still be outside your hands.
points: Turn your chest — don't lift with the hands
Head stays centered while the body coils
Slow is smooth; you're building a pattern, not speed

steps.title: Watch: the one-piece takeaway
steps.body: A 90-second demonstration. (Video drops in soon — the key positions are illustrated until then.)

steps.title: Record 3 takeaways — down the line
steps.body: Set the tripod 6 FEET BEHIND you on the target line, camera at hand height, looking at the target. In the Studio, tap the title pill to switch to Down the Line. Swing back to hip height and stop — you'll get an on-plane / inside / outside verdict on every rep.

lessons.title: Half Swings (L-to-L)
lessons.subtitle: The engine of the full swing

steps.title: The L-to-L drill
steps.body: Swing to hip height with wrists hinged into an 'L', swing through to a mirrored 'L' on the other side. This is the whole golf swing in miniature — tempo, release and balance live here.
points: Lead arm parallel to the ground, club pointing up: that's your L
Finish in a mirrored L, weight on the lead foot
Count 'one-and-two': back on 'one-and', through on 'two'

steps.title: Record 3 L-to-L swings
steps.body: Smooth and balanced beats fast. We'll score your tempo and head stability.

lessons.title: The Full Swing
lessons.subtitle: Put it all together — no ball yet

steps.title: Stretch the L-to-L
steps.body: A full swing is your half swing with more turn — not more effort. Coil the shoulders to 90°, let the wrists hinge fully, and swing through to a full, balanced finish you can hold for 3 seconds.
points: Backswing is a coil, not a lift
Start down with the lower body, not the hands
Hold the finish: chest at the target, trail toe down

steps.title: Record 3 full swings
steps.body: Practice swings, no ball. This is your first full Swing Score.

steps.title: Time to hit a ball
steps.body: Set up TrueCarry's launch monitor (landscape, tripod, behind the ball) exactly like a Range session — this app measures your ball flight while everything you've built here powers the swing.
points: Start with an 8-iron and half swings
Brush the grass — the loft does the lifting
Expect mishits: contact is a skill that arrives with reps

steps.title: Your guided Range session
steps.body: Open Play → Range and hit 10 shots. Your carry, ball speed and dispersion land in Insights automatically — and your swing scores from this track give the numbers context. Welcome to the game.
points: 10 shots, any club
Ignore distance today — watch contact and balance
Come back here after: the Slice/Hook and Contact tracks read your ball data

lessons.title: Why You Slice
lessons.subtitle: Face open to path — here's what that means

steps.title: The slice equation
steps.body: A slice = club face open to the swing path at impact. Usually the path swings out-to-in ('over the top') AND the face hangs open. We attack the face first (grip), then the path (transition).
points: Face open → ball curves right (RH)
Out-to-in path makes the curve worse and starts it left
Range mode's spin axis + HLA numbers confirm your pattern with real ball data

quiz.question: What makes the ball curve right for a right-hander?
quiz.why: Curve comes from the face-to-path gap — open face = right spin.

steps.title: Neutral vs strong grip, in 3D
checkpoints: Rotate lead hand until you see 3 knuckles
Trail hand rotates with it — palms stay facing

steps.title: Show me the stronger grip
checkpoints: Trail palm slightly under the grip

steps.title: Transition, not effort
steps.body: Over-the-top happens when the shoulders start the downswing. The fix: from the top, feel the lead hip bump toward the target FIRST while your back stays to the target a beat longer.
points: 'Bump then turn' — lower body leads
Feel the club drop behind you before it swings out
A smoother tempo gives the drop time to happen

steps.title: Record 3 transition swings — down the line
steps.body: Tripod 6 feet behind you on the target line, Studio set to Down the Line. Full practice swings — Coach calls Club On Plane / Steep / Shallow at delivery on each one.

steps.title: The hook equation
steps.body: A hook = face closed to path, usually a too-strong grip plus an in-to-out path with flippy hands. We neutralize the grip and quiet the hands with body rotation.
points: Check: do you see 4 knuckles at address? That's too strong
Body stalls → hands flip → snap hook
Keep the chest turning through impact

steps.title: The body release
steps.body: Feel your chest and hips keep rotating to the finish while the hands stay passive. The club face stays square to your body instead of snapping shut.
points: Finish with your chest at the target
Grip pressure stays constant — flips come from squeezing
Hold the finish 3 seconds

steps.title: Where the swing bottoms out
steps.body: Fat and thin shots are the same mistake: the swing's low point wandering behind the ball. The low point follows your head and pressure — keep the head centered and finish with weight left, and the bottom moves to the ball.
points: Head centered over the ball through the strike
Weight finishes 90% on the lead foot
Feel like you're brushing grass just past the ball

steps.title: Record 3 swings
steps.body: We'll track head sway and hip slide — the two low-point killers.

steps.title: Balance is a contact skill
steps.body: If you can't hold your finish, you were never in control at impact. Swing at 80% and hold every finish for a full 3 seconds.
points: 80% effort, 100% balance
Trail toe down, laces at the target
If you stumble, the swing was too fast — not too slow

steps.title: Backswing 3, downswing 1
steps.body: Nearly every great player's backswing takes ~3x as long as their downswing. Your camera measures yours to the frame at 240fps — most amateurs are 2:1 (snatchy) or 4.5:1 (slow drift then lunge).
points: Think 'low and slow' back, 'unwind' down
Speed belongs at the ball, not at takeaway
The number matters less than repeating it

steps.title: Record 3 swings for tempo
steps.body: Full practice swings at your normal effort. Target band: 2.6–3.4 : 1.

steps.title: Power flows from the ground up
steps.body: Distance isn't arm speed — it's sequence. The hips fire first and each segment slings the next like a whip. If your arms win the race, you lose the lag and the speed.
points: From the top: hip bump starts everything
Chest stays closed a beat longer
The club arrives last and fastest
